claim: 78.7% of herbal folios have a unique skeleton in position B (word immediately following a gallows marker), with lower lexicon match rates (52.6% vs 65.8%) and higher hapax (76.6% vs 67.8%) — consistent with plant-name headings behind gallows.
evidence_for: Two-way difference (lower match AND higher hapax) harder to fake
Plant names are a natural place for unique vocabulary
Structural claim independent of lexicon quality
evidence_against: Position-B effects could also reflect scribal line-beginning conventions
test: Measure hapax rate and lexicon match rate at position B vs baseline across herbal folios
test_threshold: position-B hapax >= baseline+5pp AND match rate <= baseline-5pp: confirmed.
tests_remaining: Measure hapax rate and lexicon match rate at position B vs baseline across herbal folios
reasoning: Computable from corpus + lexicon. High-value test.
source: Brady Defibaugh (April 2026), A Syriac Pharmaceutical Dispensatory Encoded in the Voynich Manuscript (DANI pipeline v31)
